finding: §8.5's per-role overlap report is defined over 'each active reviewer role', but §7.1 defines the built-in `regression` role as separate from the 'active reviewer' corpus ('...plus the built-in regression role'). Two behaviors are left unspecified: (a) whether `regression` gets its own report row / trim-candidate flag — which would contradict §5.2's 'cannot be ejected' — and (b) whether regression's findings (role=regression, non-absent) count toward corpus roles' `found_by`/`found_by_roles`, and therefore toward their `unique` vs `shared` split. Under interpretation (b-yes) a corpus role sharing only with regression has that cluster counted as `shared` (found_by=2); under (b-no) it counts as `unique`. Two testers produce contradictory unique/shared counts and contradictory report rows.
suggestion: State explicitly whether `regression` (i) appears as a reported role row and can be a trim candidate, and (ii) participates as a contributor in cluster found_by/found_by_roles. Given §5.2 (regression cannot be trimmed), the natural spec is: regression contributes to found_by but is never itself a reported row / trim candidate — say so.

finding: §3.6 says a malformed or invalid role file aborts EVERY role-reading command — explicitly including `tp init --eject-roles` — with exit 3. §5.3 says eject's `--force` 'overwrites regardless of the existing file's validity.' These directly conflict for the case an implementer must handle: an existing `.tp/reviewers/x.json` is invalid AND the user runs `tp init --eject-roles --force`. §3.6 says abort exit 3; §5.3 says overwrite it. The spec gives no rule for which wins.
suggestion: Exempt `tp init --eject-roles` (or at least its --force path) from §3.6's corpus-validation abort — eject writes the default corpus and does not need to parse existing files for emission, so `--force` should overwrite an invalid file rather than abort. Scope §3.6's exit-3 abort to the commands that actually read the corpus for emission/lint (`tp review`, `tp audit`, `tp lint`), and state eject's behavior explicitly.

finding: §3.6 states a malformed/invalid role file aborts EVERY role-reading command — explicitly including `tp init --eject-roles` — with exit 3. §5.3 states `--force` overwrites the existing role file 'regardless of the existing file's validity'. For `tp init --eject-roles --force` run over a corpus whose target file is malformed, these directly conflict: one path aborts (exit 3), the other overwrites (success). A tester cannot write a deterministic pass/fail test for this exact case.
suggestion: Carve out an explicit precedence: state that `--force` eject bypasses the §3.6 malformed-file abort for files it is about to overwrite (writes succeed), OR that §3.6's abort applies to eject only for files NOT being overwritten. Name the winning behavior and its exit code.

finding: The trim-candidate signal fires on `unique == 0` alone, but that predicate is true for EVERY reviewer in a clean or sparse round. In a zero-finding round (the convergence success state) every active role has unique=0 and shared=0, so every reviewer is flagged 'trim candidate' — 'it found nothing the others did not' is vacuously true when it found nothing at all. This is exactly backwards: the rounds that certify convergence would recommend gutting the whole panel. The §1/§8.5 'trim redundant roles' motivation is therefore incoherent on precisely the rounds that matter.
suggestion: Define a trim candidate as `unique == 0 AND shared >= 1` (contributed only overlapping findings, i.e. participated but added nothing unique). This consumes the already-computed `shared` count, automatically excludes zero-finding and no-participation rounds, and makes the flag mean 'redundant' rather than 'silent'.

finding: §8.5 requires reporting, for the round, EACH active reviewer role's unique/shared counts, and flagging any role whose unique is 0 as a trim candidate — 'it found nothing the others did not'. The prime trim candidate is a role that contributed ZERO findings this round; such a role appears in no cluster and no finding's role field. To list it (and flag unique=0), the reporter must enumerate the full active panel by loading the role corpus and applying §6.2 domain filtering. But §8.5 names three commands and two of them — `tp review --merge` (runReviewMerge takes only NDJSON file args) and `tp review --report` (resolveReportFiles takes only file/dir args) — receive no spec path and load no corpus, so they can only report roles that literally appear in the input findings. A zero-contribution role is invisible to exactly the reporters meant to surface it.
suggestion: Either give --merge/--report a spec argument (so they resolve the active panel via §6.2 and can list zero-contribution roles), or state that full-panel per-role reporting (including absent roles) is produced only by the spec-anchored `tp review <spec> --status`, and that --merge/--report report per-role counts restricted to roles present in the merged findings. Also state the data source: the per-role counts derive from each merged cluster's found_by_roles (§8.4).

finding: §8.6 redefines each merged mechanize_candidates entry as carrying `class`, `found_by`, and `signals`, and says this 'extends, does not replace' the existing output — but the existing computeMechanizeCandidates entry is {class, rounds_seen, total} with no found_by. Three things are unspecified and block implementation: (1) what `found_by` is for a class-frequency-only candidate that never formed a multi-role cluster (it has no found_by); (2) how `found_by` aggregates when one class spans multiple distinct (location,class) clusters in a single round (§8.3 makes clusters per-location, so class C can yield several clusters with different found_by — max? sum? which?); (3) whether the existing rounds_seen/total fields are retained, dropped, or folded into the new schema.
suggestion: Define the full merged entry schema explicitly: e.g. keep {class, rounds_seen, total} and add {found_by, signals}; define found_by for a class as the MAX found_by across that class's clusters in the round (or 0/omitted when only class-frequency fired); state that signals is the set of {multi-role, class-frequency} triggers that fired for that class.

finding: §8.5 flags any active reviewer role with unique=0 as a 'trim candidate'. The built-in `regression` role is emitted every round (§7.1) and its findings carry role=regression (§7.3), so it participates in clusters and can end a round with unique=0. But §5.2 says regression 'cannot be ejected' and is not a corpus file — it is untrimmable. The spec never states whether regression is included in the per-role overlap report; if it is, tp would flag as a trim candidate a role that cannot be trimmed (a misleading, unactionable signal).
suggestion: State explicitly whether regression is included in the §8.5 per-role report. Recommend excluding regression from trim-candidate flagging (report its unique/shared for information only, never as a trim candidate), since §5.2 forbids trimming it.

finding: Cluster representative = highest-severity finding, ties broken by lexicographic `role`. Two findings that share the same location key, class, severity AND role have no defined final tiebreak, so which one becomes representative — and thus the merged output's displayed `finding` text and other free-text fields — is nondeterministic. Since `tp review --merge` emits the representative's text, two conforming implementations produce different merged output for identical input, defeating a deterministic golden-output test (tp otherwise guarantees deterministic output).
suggestion: Add a total final tiebreak after (severity, role), e.g. lexicographic on the finding text (or on a stable input order index), so the representative — and the merged output — is fully deterministic.

finding: §3.6 says validation 'aborts every role-reading command (tp review, tp audit, tp lint, tp init --eject-roles)' 'whenever a .tp/reviewers/ or .tp/auditors/ directory exists'. It does not state whether a command validates only the phase it emits or the whole corpus. Concretely: `tp review` with a valid `.tp/reviewers/` but a malformed `.tp/auditors/x.json` — abort with exit 3, or succeed? The prose supports both readings ('every role-reading command' + 'the role corpus' → whole-corpus abort; operationally review never reads auditors → phase-scoped). Two testers write opposite expectations.
suggestion: State per-command validation scope: e.g. 'tp review validates only .tp/reviewers/, tp audit only .tp/auditors/, tp lint both; a malformed file in a phase a command does not read does not abort that command.'

finding: §8.6 merges multi-role clusters into 'one list keyed on `class`' alongside the existing cross-round class-frequency candidates, and says 'each entry carries class, found_by, and signals.' But found_by is a per-cluster property scoped to (location, class): a single class can span several location-clusters, each with its own found_by, and 'found_by' for a multi-role cluster (distinct roles in one round) is a different quantity than the class-frequency count (occurrences across rounds). Collapsing to one class-keyed entry with a single `found_by` field conflates these and loses the per-location counts; when BOTH signals fire, which number `found_by` holds is undefined.
suggestion: Specify how found_by is derived for a class-keyed entry (e.g. max across that class's location-clusters, or a separate field per signal), and state that the class-frequency count and the multi-role role-count are distinct values rather than a single overloaded `found_by`.

finding: §8.4 defines found_by_roles as the 'sorted set of distinct non-absent contributing roles', which includes the built-in `regression` role (it stamps role=regression, a non-absent value). §8.5 then derives each active reviewer role's `unique`/`shared` from cluster membership: a reviewer whose findings overlap only with regression sees found_by>=2, so those clusters count as `shared` not `unique`, potentially zeroing the reviewer's `unique` and flagging it a trim candidate. The spec never states whether regression's contributions count toward the reviewer uniqueness computation — §7.1 pointedly separates 'active reviewer role' from 'the built-in regression role', while §8.4 folds regression into found_by_roles. The two readings produce observably different trim-candidate flags, and one of them names distinct reviewers trimmable purely because the un-trimmable built-in also caught the finding.
suggestion: State explicitly in §8.5 (or §8.4) whether the built-in regression role counts toward reviewer roles' found_by_roles / unique / shared. Either exclude regression from the per-role uniqueness computation (so a reviewer is only 'shared' when another user role overlaps it), or affirm it counts and document that a reviewer redundant with regression is a valid trim signal.

finding: §3.6 says 'a malformed or invalid role file aborts every role-reading command (tp review, tp audit, ...)'. It does not state whether the abort is phase-scoped. tp review emits only reviewer prompts and tp audit only auditor prompts, and §9.2 stresses the review and audit sequences are independent. Under a literal reading, a broken `.tp/auditors/x.json` would abort `tp review` (exit 3) even though review never uses auditors — which contradicts the phase-independence §9.2 asserts. Two implementers could reasonably diverge (global vs phase-scoped abort).
suggestion: State that each command validates and aborts only on its own phase's corpus: `tp review` aborts on a malformed reviewer file, `tp audit` on a malformed auditor file, and `tp lint` (which validates both) on either. Keep this consistent with §9.2's independent sequences.

finding: §8.4 counts `found_by_roles` as all distinct non-absent contributing roles, which includes the built-in `regression` role (it emits review findings with role=regression). So a genuine reviewer whose findings all coincide with regression's shows unique=0 and is flagged a trim candidate — yet regression is non-ejectable (§5.2) and only covers regressions, so trimming that reviewer loses its distinct lens while the 'overlap' cannot be removed. Separately, §8.5 reports 'each active reviewer role', but §7.1 defines the active reviewer panel as the corpus roles 'plus the built-in regression role', leaving it unstated whether regression itself gets a report row (and a meaningless 'trim candidate: regression' the user cannot act on).
suggestion: Compute unique/shared over the trimmable panel only: exclude regression from the found_by used to decide other roles' uniqueness, and exclude regression from its own trim-candidate flagging. State this explicitly in §8.5 cross-referencing §5.2/§7.1.

finding: The unified mechanize_candidates list is 'keyed on class', yet `found_by` is only defined for the multi-role signal (a single cluster's found_by, §8.4). Three cases have no defined `found_by` value: (1) an entry where only the class-frequency signal fired (no qualifying cluster exists — is found_by the cross-round class occurrence count, or 0/absent?); (2) a class with multiple distinct clusters (different location keys) each with its own found_by — which one does the class-keyed entry report (max, sum, first?); (3) an entry where both signals fire (multi-role found_by vs class-frequency count). A tester cannot predict the emitted `found_by` for a class-keyed entry.
suggestion: Define `found_by` for the merged class-keyed entry precisely, e.g. 'the maximum found_by across that class's qualifying clusters (0 when only the class-frequency signal fired)', and state which count `signals:class-frequency` corresponds to.

finding: §9.1 hashes each file's raw bytes and claims 'A repo-relative path keeps the hash identical across clones.' The path component is clone-stable, but the raw bytes are not: git line-ending normalization (core.autocrlf on a Windows checkout, or .gitattributes eol settings) rewrites the working-tree bytes tp reads, so two clones of the same commit produce different review_roles_hash values and one teammate sees spurious `roles_stale`. This defeats §4.3's goal that convergence 'travel with the repo'. The 'identical across clones' guarantee holds only for the path, not for the whole hash.
suggestion: Either hash a normalized byte form (LF-normalize before hashing) so the guarantee is real, or narrow the §9.1 claim to state that clone-identity assumes byte-identical checkouts (no autocrlf/eol rewriting) and accept that cross-platform reformatting costs one re-confirming round like eject (§5.4).

finding: §4.4 states a populated phase directory 'replaces — does not merge with — the embedded default corpus ... the active corpus is exactly its files' as an absolute rule, with no cross-reference to the §6.2 exception. §6.2 carves out that when domain filtering empties the panel, tp 'falls back to that phase's full domain-selected embedded corpus' — i.e. it emits embedded roles the project explicitly replaced. An implementer reading §4.4 alone would not expect embedded roles to ever emit when user files exist.
suggestion: Add a pointer in §4.4 to the §6.2 empty-panel fallback, or qualify 'exactly its files' as the pre-domain-filter corpus definition, distinguishing the corpus (files) from the emitted panel (post-filter, with the §6.2 safety fallback).

finding: The cluster representative is 'the highest-severity finding in a cluster (ties broken by lexicographic role)'. A single role can emit two findings that land in the same cluster (same location key and class), e.g. implementer files two '§8 / consistency' findings at equal severity. Then the severity tie AND the role tie both resolve equal, leaving the representative — which 'supplies its displayed fields' — undefined. This conflicts with tp's deterministic-behavior principle (identical inputs must yield identical output).
suggestion: Add a final stable tie-break to §8.4 after severity and role — e.g. lexicographic finding text, or original input order — so the representative is fully determined even when severity and role are equal.

finding: §8.6 introduces MECHANIZE_QUORUM (a named constant, value 2) to select multi-role mechanize candidates (found_by >= 2). This is definitionally identical to §8.5's `shared` predicate (clusters with found_by >= 2). Two names for the same boundary is a small over-abstraction — a knob no decision distinguishes from `shared` (Principle 4: 'no metric no decision consumes').
suggestion: Drop MECHANIZE_QUORUM and define the multi-role mechanize signal directly as 'a shared cluster (found_by >= 2)', reusing the §8.5 concept, unless a future need to diverge the two thresholds is demonstrated.
